feat(harness): add CI gate matrix with flake budgets and auto-bisect hooks (bd-1dp9.7.3)

Implements CI lane definitions, per-lane/global flake budget enforcement,
auto-bisect trigger logic, and artifact publication manifests. Includes
27 unit tests and 5 E2E tests. Also stages concurrent agent work
(e2e_logging_init, MVCC EBR, vectorized batch fixes).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-14vp7","title":"Vectorized Execution + Morsel-Driven Parallelism (§8.2)","description":"Replace row-at-a-time VDBE interpretation with vectorized batch processing and morsel-driven parallel execution. This is scored 10.0 in the opportunity matrix — the single largest known performance uplift available.

## Background
SQLite's VDBE processes one row at a time with significant per-row interpretation overhead (opcode dispatch, type checking, memory allocation). Modern analytical engines (DuckDB, HyPer, MonetDB/X100) process data in columnar batches of 1024-4096 rows, amortizing interpretation overhead and enabling SIMD vectorization.

## Mathematical Foundation
Boncz et al. 2005 (MonetDB/X100) showed that vectorized execution achieves near-hardware-limit throughput by keeping data in CPU caches. Leis et al. 2014 (morsel-driven parallelism) showed that splitting work into cache-sized 'morsels' and dispatching to worker threads achieves near-linear scaling with cores while maintaining low scheduling overhead.

## Key Papers
- Boncz, Zukowski, Nes 2005: 'MonetDB/X100: Hyper-Pipelining Query Execution'
- Leis, Boncz, Kemper, Neumann 2014: 'Morsel-Driven Parallelism: A NUMA-Aware Query Evaluation Framework'
- Kersten, Leis, Kemper, Neumann, Pavlo 2018: 'Everything You Always Wanted to Know About Compiled and Vectorized Query Engines'

## Implementation Plan
1. Define columnar batch format (Arrow-compatible): arrays of typed values, validity bitmaps for NULLs
2. Vectorized operators: scan, filter, project, hash-join, aggregation, sort
3. SIMD primitives for filter evaluation, hash computation, comparison
4. Morsel dispatcher: partition table scans into morsels, assign to worker threads via work-stealing
5. Pipeline breakers (hash-join build, sort) use parallel partitioned algorithms

## Expected Impact
- 5-20x throughput improvement on analytical queries (scan-heavy, filter-heavy)
- Near-linear scaling with CPU cores for parallel queries
- Reduced memory bandwidth pressure through columnar layout

## Connection to Other Features
- Leapfrog Triejoin can emit vectorized batches for downstream operators
- SSI read-set tracking must handle batch-level granularity
- Buffer pool (S3-FIFO) feeds vectorized scan with page-aligned reads
- EBR epoch management aligns with morsel boundaries

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Columnar batch format defined with Arrow compatibility
- [ ] All core operators vectorized (scan, filter, join, agg, sort)
- [ ] SIMD acceleration for filter and hash operations
- [ ] Morsel-driven parallel dispatcher with work-stealing
- [ ] TPC-H queries run end-to-end through vectorized engine
- [ ] Benchmark: 5x+ improvement over interpreter on TPC-H SF1","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:17:38.265137567Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:17:38.265137567Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-concept","execution-engine","performance"]}
 {"id":"bd-14vp7.1","title":"Design vectorized batch format (columnar, 1024-row batches)","description":"Design the core columnar batch format for vectorized execution. Each batch contains 1024 rows (tunable) stored in columnar layout: one array per column. Design considerations:

1. Column types: integers (i8/i16/i32/i64), floats (f32/f64), blobs, text (offset+data), NULL bitmap
2. Memory layout: aligned arrays for SIMD (32-byte or 64-byte alignment)
3. Validity bitmap: one bit per row per column for NULL tracking
4. Selection vector: indices of 'active' rows (for filter pushdown without copying)
5. Ownership: arena-allocated per-batch, freed as a unit
6. Arrow compatibility: layout should be zero-copy convertible to/from Arrow arrays

The batch format is the fundamental data exchange unit between all vectorized operators. Getting this right is critical — it determines SIMD compatibility, cache behavior, and memory efficiency.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Batch format specification document
- [ ] Rust type definitions for Batch, Column, SelectionVector
- [ ] Construction from row-oriented input (for compatibility)
- [ ] Zero-copy Arrow conversion (both directions)
- [ ] Micro-benchmark: batch construction throughput
- [ ] Memory alignment verified for SIMD operations","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:17:50.057650596Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:49:24.153594148Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T09:49:24.153572818Z","close_reason":"Implemented vectorized batch format module, row->batch builder, Arrow-compatible zero-copy interchange, alignment verification, benchmark, and spec doc","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-concept","design","execution-engine"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-14vp7.1","depends_on_id":"bd-14vp7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:17:50.057650596Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"bd-14vp7.2","title":"Implement vectorized scan operator","description":"Implement the vectorized table scan operator that reads pages from the buffer pool and produces columnar batches. The scan operator:

1. Reads B-tree leaf pages sequentially through the buffer pool
2. Transcodes row-oriented page data into columnar batch format
3. Applies early filter pushdown via selection vectors (avoid full materialization)
4. Supports morsel boundaries: each morsel is a contiguous range of pages
5. Prefetches next pages while processing current batch (async I/O or prefetch hints)

This is the 'source' operator — all vectorized pipelines start with a scan. Performance here directly determines maximum throughput since scan is the bottleneck for analytical queries.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Scan produces correct columnar batches from B-tree pages
- [ ] Filter pushdown via selection vectors works
- [ ] Morsel boundaries respected (scan can be split across threads)
- [ ] Prefetch integration with buffer pool
- [ ] Benchmark: scan throughput (GB/s) for sequential reads
- [ ] Correctness: scan output matches row-at-a-time output","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:17:59.791991532Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:50:21.631567478Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-concept","execution-engine"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-14vp7.2","depends_on_id":"bd-14vp7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:17:59.791991532Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-14vp7.2","depends_on_id":"bd-14vp7.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:16.930471959Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-14vp7.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1xfri","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:40.899337117Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-14vp7.3","title":"Implement vectorized filter operator with SIMD","description":"Implement the vectorized filter operator that evaluates predicates over columnar batches using SIMD instructions. For each batch:

1. Evaluate predicate expression over column arrays (e.g., col_a > 42 AND col_b < 100)
2. Produce a selection vector (bitmap or index list) of matching rows
3. Compose with existing selection vectors (AND of filters)
4. Use SIMD intrinsics for comparison operations: _mm256_cmpgt_epi32, etc.
5. Runtime SIMD detection: AVX2 fast path, SSE2 fallback, scalar fallback

Key SIMD patterns:
- Integer comparison: 8 x i32 per 256-bit register
- String prefix comparison: PCMPESTRI for short strings
- NULL handling: AND with validity bitmap before comparison
- Branching elimination: use SIMD masks instead of branch-per-row

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Filter evaluates arithmetic comparisons over integer columns
- [ ] SIMD acceleration with AVX2/SSE2 paths
- [ ] NULL handling correct (NULL comparisons produce NULL/false)
- [ ] Selection vector composition works for AND/OR predicates
- [ ] Benchmark: filter throughput (rows/s) vs scalar baseline
- [ ] Correctness: filter output matches row-at-a-time evaluation","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:18:09.592948861Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:17.257794428Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-concept","execution-engine","simd"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-14vp7.3","depends_on_id":"bd-14vp7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:18:09.592948861Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-14vp7.3","depends_on_id":"bd-14vp7.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:17.257730147Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-14vp7.4","title":"Implement vectorized hash-join operator","description":"Implement the vectorized hash-join operator for two-relation equi-joins (Leapfrog handles 3+). Two phases:

BUILD phase:
1. Scan build side into columnar batches
2. Hash join keys using vectorized hash function (wyhash or xxhash, SIMD-friendly)
3. Insert into a partitioned hash table (open addressing, Robin Hood or linear probing)
4. Parallel build: each thread builds a local partition, then merge

PROBE phase:
1. For each probe batch, compute hash of join keys (vectorized)
2. Gather-probe: use SIMD gather to look up hash table entries
3. Produce output batch with matching pairs
4. Handle hash collisions with vectorized comparison

The hash table must be cache-friendly: keys and payloads stored separately to maximize cache line utilization during probing.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Build phase correctly constructs hash table from batches
- [ ] Probe phase produces correct join output
- [ ] Vectorized hash computation
- [ ] Parallel build with partition merge
- [ ] Inner join, left outer join, semi join, anti join variants
- [ ] Benchmark: hash-join throughput vs row-at-a-time
- [ ] Correctness: output matches row-at-a-time hash join","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:18:20.281625224Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:17.585364811Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-concept","execution-engine"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-14vp7.4","depends_on_id":"bd-14vp7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:18:20.281625224Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-14vp7.4","depends_on_id":"bd-14vp7.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:17.585277407Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-14vp7.5","title":"Implement vectorized aggregation operator","description":"Implement the vectorized aggregation operator for GROUP BY queries. Two modes:

HASH aggregation (general case):
1. Hash group-by keys (vectorized)
2. Lookup/insert into aggregation hash table
3. Update aggregate states in batch (SUM: SIMD horizontal add, COUNT: popcount on validity bitmap, MIN/MAX: SIMD min/max, AVG: running sum + count)
4. Parallel: per-thread local aggregation tables, merge at pipeline end

ORDERED aggregation (when input is sorted on group-by keys):
1. Detect group boundaries via vectorized comparison
2. Aggregate within each group contiguously
3. No hash table needed — streaming output

Aggregate functions to support: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX, GROUP_CONCAT, total, count(distinct). Each aggregate has init/update/merge/finalize methods operating on columnar arrays.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Hash aggregation produces correct results for all standard aggregate functions
- [ ] Ordered aggregation path for sorted input
- [ ] SIMD acceleration for SUM, COUNT, MIN, MAX
- [ ] Parallel aggregation with merge
- [ ] NULL handling correct for all aggregates
- [ ] Benchmark: aggregation throughput vs row-at-a-time
- [ ] Correctness: output matches row-at-a-time for TPC-H aggregate queries","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:18:30.488918509Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:17.920154827Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-concept","execution-engine"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-14vp7.5","depends_on_id":"bd-14vp7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:18:30.488918509Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-14vp7.5","depends_on_id":"bd-14vp7.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:17.920054129Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"bd-14vp7.6","title":"Implement morsel-driven parallel dispatcher","description":"Implement the morsel-driven parallel execution framework per Leis et al. 2014. Core concepts:

MORSEL: A contiguous chunk of input data (e.g., 10000 rows or a range of pages). Small enough to fit in L2/L3 cache.

PIPELINE: A sequence of operators that can execute without materializing intermediate results (e.g., scan -> filter -> project -> hash-join-probe -> aggregate-update).

PIPELINE BREAKER: An operator that requires full materialization of its input before producing output (hash-join-build, sort, aggregation-finalize).

DISPATCHER: 
1. Partition input into morsels
2. Create pipeline tasks (one per morsel)
3. Worker threads pull tasks from a work-stealing deque (crossbeam)
4. NUMA-awareness: prefer morsels from local NUMA node
5. Dynamic load balancing: fast threads steal from slow threads
6. Pipeline barriers: all morsels of current pipeline must complete before next pipeline starts

Thread pool: fixed number of workers (default: num_cpus - 1 for query threads). One coordinator thread manages pipeline progression.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Morsel partitioning for table scans
- [ ] Pipeline definition and execution framework
- [ ] Work-stealing dispatcher with crossbeam deques
- [ ] Pipeline barriers between pipeline breakers
- [ ] Near-linear scaling: 2x threads -> ~1.8x throughput (min 90% efficiency)
- [ ] NUMA-awareness (at least locality hints)
- [ ] Benchmark: parallel TPC-H Q1 (scan+filter+agg) scaling to 8 cores
- [ ] No data races (Miri, ThreadSanitizer clean)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:18:43.694026899Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:18.243271398Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-concept","execution-engine","parallelism"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-14vp7.6","depends_on_id":"bd-14vp7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:18:43.694026899Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-14vp7.6","depends_on_id":"bd-14vp7.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:18.243142046Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-14vp7.7","title":"Benchmark: vectorized vs interpreter on TPC-H","description":"Comprehensive benchmark comparing vectorized execution engine against the existing VDBE interpreter on TPC-H queries. This is the definitive proof that vectorization delivers the expected 5-20x improvement.

Queries: Full TPC-H suite (Q1-Q22) at SF1, SF10.

Metrics per query:
- Wall-clock latency (p50, p95, p99 over 10 runs)
- Throughput (rows/second)
- CPU cycles per row (perf stat)
- L1/L2/L3 cache miss rates
- Branch misprediction rates
- Memory bandwidth utilization
- Peak memory usage
- Flamegraph artifacts

Comparison dimensions:
1. Interpreter (baseline)
2. Vectorized, single-threaded
3. Vectorized, 2 threads
4. Vectorized, 4 threads
5. Vectorized, 8 threads

Expected results:
- Single-threaded vectorized: 5-10x over interpreter (cache + SIMD effects)
- 8-thread vectorized: 30-80x over interpreter (parallelism multiplier)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All TPC-H queries run on both engines producing identical results
- [ ] Performance metrics captured for all comparison dimensions
- [ ] Scaling plot: speedup vs thread count
- [ ] Flamegraphs for top-3 bottleneck queries
- [ ] CI integration: SF1 regression detection (5% threshold)
- [ ] Results documented in benchmark report","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:18:55.305010677Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:20.087337256Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-concept","benchmark","execution-engine"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-14vp7.7","depends_on_id":"bd-14vp7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:18:55.305010677Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-14vp7.7","depends_on_id":"bd-14vp7.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:18.829443695Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-14vp7.7","depends_on_id":"bd-14vp7.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:19.148764695Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-14vp7.7","depends_on_id":"bd-14vp7.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:19.464320463Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-14vp7.7","depends_on_id":"bd-14vp7.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:19.776433712Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-14vp7.7","depends_on_id":"bd-14vp7.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:20.087262766Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-14zc","title":"5E.1: Wire BEGIN CONCURRENT through MVCC ConcurrentRegistry","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:52:03.049602990Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T09:11:35.495837937Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T09:11:35.495816286Z","close_reason":"5E.1 complete: BEGIN CONCURRENT lifecycle wired through ConcurrentRegistry. Tests passing: test_begin_concurrent_*, test_concurrent_session_lifecycle, test_snapshot_isolation_*, test_concurrent_read_local_vs_mvcc. Commit/rollback paths route through concurrent_commit/concurrent_abort. Page-level locking during writes is 5E.2 scope (bd-kivg).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-14zc","depends_on_id":"bd-ujkm","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:21:31.471600088Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1282,"issue_id":"bd-14zc","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# 5E.1: Wire BEGIN CONCURRENT through MVCC ConcurrentRegistry

## What

When a user issues `BEGIN CONCURRENT`, the Connection must engage the MVCC concurrent writer
path instead of falling through to the serialized (global write mutex) path. This means:

1. Creating a `ConcurrentHandle` via `ConcurrentRegistry.begin_concurrent(snapshot)`
2. Storing the session_id so subsequent DML can route through `concurrent_write_page()`
3. Ensuring reads during the transaction check the local write set first via `concurrent_read_page()`
4. Routing commit through `concurrent_commit()` instead of the serialized commit path

## Current state

In `crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs` (lines ~1603-1638), `execute_begin()`:
- Recognizes `TransactionMode::Concurrent` from the AST
- Sets `self.concurrent_txn` flag to true
- But maps it to `pager::TransactionMode::Deferred` — which is the serialized path
- The `ConcurrentRegistry` in `crates/fsqlite-mvcc/src/begin_concurrent.rs` is fully
  implemented but never invoked from Connection

The `ConcurrentRegistry` API (lines 151-211 of begin_concurrent.rs):
```rust
registry.begin_concurrent(snapshot) -> Result<u64, MvccError>  // Returns session_id
registry.get(session_id) -> Option<&ConcurrentHandle>
registry.get_mut(session_id) -> Option<&mut ConcurrentHandle>
registry.remove(session_id) -> Option<ConcurrentHandle>
```

## Where to change

**Primary:** `crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs` — `execute_begin()`, add concurrent session state

**Secondary:** `crates/fsqlite-pager/src/pager.rs` — `begin()` must accept `TransactionMode::Concurrent`
and route through ConcurrentRegistry instead of SerializedWriteMutex

## Implementation approach

1. Add `concurrent_session_id: RefCell<Option<u64>>` to Connection (or store in pager)
2. When `execute_begin()` sees `TransactionMode::Concurrent`:
   - Establish read snapshot via pager
   - Call `ConcurrentRegistry.begin_concurrent(snapshot)` → get session_id
   - Store session_id for use by DML operations
3. The TransactionHandle returned by pager.begin() must be aware it's concurrent:
   - `write_page()` routes through `concurrent_write_page()` (acquires page lock, records in write set)
   - `get_page()` routes through `concurrent_read_page()` (checks write set first, then version store)
4. `execute_commit()` must route through `concurrent_commit()` which does first-committer-wins validation
5. `execute_rollback()` must call `concurrent_abort()` to release page locks

## Key types involved

- `ConcurrentRegistry` (begin_concurrent.rs:151) — registry of active sessions
- `ConcurrentHandle` (begin_concurrent.rs:54) — per-session state
- `concurrent_write_page()` (begin_concurrent.rs:218) — write + acquire lock
- `concurrent_read_page()` (begin_concurrent.rs:244) — read from write set
- `concurrent_commit()` (begin_concurrent.rs:288) — FCW validation + commit
- `concurrent_abort()` (begin_concurrent.rs:322) — release locks + abort
- `InProcessPageLockTable` (core_types.rs:191) — page-level locks
- `CommitIndex` (core_types.rs:927) — page → CommitSeq mapping
- `MAX_CONCURRENT_WRITERS = 128` — soft limit, returns SQLITE_BUSY if exceeded

## C SQLite reference

C SQLite's BEGIN CONCURRENT (from the begin-concurrent branch) establishes a read snapshot
and defers writer exclusion until commit time. Our implementation goes further: page-level
locks are acquired eagerly during DML (not deferred to commit), enabling earlier conflict
detection. First-committer-wins validation at commit checks the CommitIndex.

## Tests

- `test_begin_concurrent_creates_session`: BEGIN CONCURRENT → session_id exists in registry
- `test_concurrent_txn_reads_own_writes`: BEGIN CONCURRENT, INSERT, SELECT → sees inserted row
- `test_concurrent_txn_snapshot_isolation`: Writer A inserts, Writer B (concurrent) doesn't see it
- `test_concurrent_commit_updates_commit_index`: COMMIT → CommitIndex updated for written pages
- `test_concurrent_rollback_releases_locks`: ROLLBACK → all page locks released
- `test_max_concurrent_writers`: Open 128 concurrent txns → 129th returns SQLITE_BUSY

## CRITICAL RULE (from AGENTS.md)

`concurrent_mode_default` is `true` in `Connection`. This means **every `BEGIN` is promoted
to `BEGIN CONCURRENT`** unless the user explicitly opts out with `BEGIN DEFERRED`, `BEGIN
IMMEDIATE`, or `BEGIN EXCLUSIVE`.

This rule is the entire reason FrankenSQLite exists. 5E.1 wires the concurrent path, but
the default promotion to CONCURRENT is ALREADY in place (the flag is set). What's missing
is the actual MVCC machinery being engaged when the flag is active.

## Impact on 5E.1 implementation

The current code in `execute_begin()` (connection.rs:~1603-1638):
- Checks `concurrent_mode_default` flag → true by default
- When true and no explicit mode given: promotes to `Concurrent`
- BUT maps `Concurrent` to `pager::TransactionMode::Deferred` (serialized path!)

The fix is: when mode is `Concurrent`, call `ConcurrentRegistry.begin_concurrent(snapshot)`
instead of falling through to the serialized `pager.begin(Deferred)`.

## Key locations that MUST stay concurrent_mode_default = true

Per AGENTS.md:
- `crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs` — `concurrent_mode_default: RefCell::new(true)`
- `crates/fsqlite-e2e/src/lib.rs` — `HarnessSettings::default()` → `concurrent_mode: true`
- `crates/fsqlite-e2e/src/fsqlite_executor.rs` — `FsqliteExecConfig::default()` → `concurrent_mode: true`
- `crates/fsqlite-e2e/src/fairness.rs` — `benchmark_settings()` → `concurrent_mode: true`

**5E.1 must NOT change any of these defaults.** The existing promotion logic stays; we just
make the concurrent path actually DO something instead of falling through to serialized.

## Additional test

- `test_begin_without_mode_uses_concurrent`: `BEGIN` (no mode specified) → concurrent_txn=true, ConcurrentHandle created

 {"id":"bd-151x","title":"5G.1: Execute CREATE TRIGGER statements (store trigger definition in schema)","description":"## What

Route `CreateTriggerStatement` (already fully parsed by `parser.rs:parse_create_trigger()`) through the `execute()` dispatcher in `connection.rs`, storing the trigger definition in the in-memory schema and persisting it to sqlite_master.

## Current Behavior

`CREATE TRIGGER` hits the wildcard `_ =>` arm in `execute()` (connection.rs ~line 776) and returns `FrankenError::NotImplemented`. `DROP TRIGGER` silently no-ops (line 1288-1291). Parser support is complete — all syntax is accepted: BEFORE/AFTER/INSTEAD OF, INSERT/DELETE/UPDATE [OF column-list], FOR EACH ROW, WHEN clause, multi-statement body in BEGIN...END.

## Implementation

### 1. Add `TriggerDef` to schema representation

```rust
pub struct TriggerDef {
    pub name: String,
    pub table_name: String,
    pub timing: TriggerTiming,       // Before, After, InsteadOf
    pub event: TriggerEvent,         // Insert, Delete, Update(Vec<String>)
    pub for_each_row: bool,          // always true in SQLite
    pub when_clause: Option<Expr>,   // parsed AST expression
    pub body: Vec<Statement>,        // parsed DML statements
    pub create_sql: String,          // original CREATE TRIGGER SQL for sqlite_master
}
```

Store in a `Vec<TriggerDef>` alongside the existing schema tables vector in `Connection`.

### 2. Route through execute()

Add a match arm in `execute()` for `Statement::CreateTrigger(stmt)`:
- Validate the target table exists in schema
- Check for duplicate trigger name
- Build `TriggerDef` from the parsed AST
- Append to the triggers vector
- Insert into sqlite_master: `INSERT INTO sqlite_master VALUES ('trigger', name, tbl_name, 0, sql)`

### 3. Handle IF NOT EXISTS

If `stmt.if_not_exists` is true and trigger already exists, silently succeed (no error).

### 4. Handle TEMP triggers

TEMP triggers go into the temp schema, not persistent sqlite_master. Store separately or flag in TriggerDef.

## Files to Modify

- `crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs` — New match arm in `execute()`, TriggerDef struct, triggers vector
- `crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs` — Schema loading (5A.4 will need to load triggers from sqlite_master)

## Tests (in connection.rs tests module)

- `test_create_trigger_basic` — CREATE TRIGGER succeeds, verify trigger exists in schema
- `test_create_trigger_if_not_exists` — Duplicate with IF NOT EXISTS succeeds silently
- `test_create_trigger_duplicate_name_errors` — Duplicate without IF NOT EXISTS returns error
- `test_create_trigger_nonexistent_table_errors` — Target table must exist
- `test_create_trigger_all_timings` — BEFORE, AFTER, INSTEAD OF all accepted
- `test_create_trigger_all_events` — INSERT, DELETE, UPDATE, UPDATE OF col accepted
- `test_create_trigger_with_when_clause` — WHEN expr stored correctly
- `test_create_trigger_persists_to_sqlite_master` — Row appears in sqlite_master with type='trigger'","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T03:47:09.851494672Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T08:53:40.837234685Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T08:53:40.837206532Z","close_reason":"Implemented CREATE TRIGGER execution in connection dispatcher, added TriggerDef in-memory schema registry, persisted non-TEMP triggers to sqlite_master, reloaded triggers from sqlite_master on open, and added connection-level tests for basic create/if-not-exists/nonexistent-table/WHEN-clause/round-trip persistence.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-151x","depends_on_id":"bd-19xv","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T03:47:40.336015350Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-151x","depends_on_id":"bd-oidd","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T03:47:29.132305137Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

This task now owns CI gate/artifact obligations directly within PARITY-100 while staying compatible with any shared CI hardening outputs.","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:12:42Z"},{"id":1466,"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.7.3","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## bd-1dp9.7.3 Evidence: CI gate matrix, flake budgets, auto-bisect hooks, artifact publication

### Agent: AmberPeak

### Implementation

New module: `crates/fsqlite-harness/src/ci_gate_matrix.rs`

Four components delivered:

#### 1. CI Lane Definitions
- `CiLane` enum: Unit, E2eDifferential, E2eCorrectness, E2eRecovery, Performance, SchemaValidation, CoverageDrift
- `CiLane::ALL` constant for iteration
- `supports_retry()` — lanes with deterministic replay support flake detection

#### 2. Flake Budget Policy
- `FlakeBudgetPolicy` with per-lane `LaneFlakeBudget` (max_flake_rate, max_retries, blocking/advisory)
- `evaluate_flake_budget()` — computes flake rate, compares against budget, determines pipeline_fail
- `evaluate_global_flake_budget()` — aggregates across all lanes with global rate limit
- `GlobalFlakeBudgetResult::render_summary()` — CI-friendly human-readable output
- Performance lane is advisory (blocking=false, 10% budget); all others are blocking (5% budget)

#### 3. Auto-Bisect Hooks
- `BisectTrigger` enum: GateRegression, PerformanceRegression, DriftShiftDetected
- `should_trigger_bisect()` — evaluates lane result + config to determine trigger
- `build_bisect_request()` — constructs BisectRequest with commit range, replay seed, reproduction command
- `AutoBisectConfig` with enabled flag, max_steps, step_timeout, eligible lanes

#### 4. Artifact Publication
- `ArtifactManifest` with schema_version, run_id, lane, git_sha, seed, artifacts, gate_passed, bisect_request
- `ArtifactEntry` with kind (Log/Report/Manifest/Database/Trace/Benchmark), path, content_hash, size_bytes
- `ArtifactManifest::validate()` — catches empty fields and invalid checksums
- `build_artifact_manifest()` — constructor with full artifact set
- JSON-serializable for CI artifact publishing

### Test Evidence

**27 unit tests, all passing:**

```
cargo test -p fsqlite-harness --lib ci_gate_matrix
test result: ok. 27 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored
```

**5 E2E tests, all passing:**

```
cargo test -p fsqlite-harness --test bd_1dp9_7_3_ci_gate_matrix
test result: ok. 5 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored
```

E2E test breakdown:
- e2e_full_pipeline_pass_all_lanes — all 7 lanes pass, no bisect triggered
- e2e_full_pipeline_regression_triggers_bisect — hard failure triggers GateRegression bisect
- e2e_flake_budget_mixed_lanes — mixed blocking/advisory lanes, global over-budget detection
- e2e_artifact_manifest_multi_artifact — 4-artifact manifest validation and JSON roundtrip
- e2e_pipeline_determinism — byte-identical output across repeated evaluations

### Quality Gates
- `cargo fmt --check` — clean
- `cargo clippy -p fsqlite-harness --lib` — zero issues in ci_gate_matrix.rs
- All 32 tests deterministic and reproducible

### Acceptance Criteria Coverage
1. CI matrix with unit/e2e/differential/perf gates — YES (7 lanes defined)
2. Artifact publishing with checksums — YES (ArtifactManifest with validate())
3. Deterministic retries and flake budget policies — YES (per-lane + global flake budgets)
4. Auto-bisect hooks — YES (trigger detection + BisectRequest construction)

","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:12:34Z"},{"id":1460,"issue_id":"bd-1dp9.7.6","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## bd-1dp9.7.6 Evidence: Structured log schema validator, redaction policy, and replay-decoder testpack

### Agent: AmberPeak

### Implementation

New module: `crates/fsqlite-harness/src/log_schema_validator.rs`

Three components delivered:

#### 1. Batch Schema Validator
- `validate_event_stream()` — processes event sequences, returns `ValidationReport` with per-event diagnostics
- Wraps existing `validate_log_event()` from bd-1dp9.7.2 with extended checks:
  - Correlation ID format validation (run_id should follow `{bead_id}-{timestamp}-{pid}`)
  - Recommended field warnings (scenario_id, seed)
  - Context requirements for first_divergence events
  - artifact_hash requirement for artifact_generated events
- `ValidationReport` with CI-friendly `passed` flag, aggregate stats, and `render_summary()` for human-readable output
- Diagnostic severity levels: Error (schema violation), Warning (quality issue), Info (recommendation)
- JSON-serializable report for CI artifact publishing

#### 2. Deterministic Redaction Policy
- `FieldSensitivity` classification: Safe / Internal / Sensitive
- `RedactionStrategy` enum: Preserve / DeterministicHash / FixedPlaceholder / PathBasename
- `build_field_classifications()` — canonical classification for all schema fields
- `classify_field()` — lookup with wildcard matching for context.* keys
- `redact_event()` — deterministic redaction using xxhash (same salt + same input = same output)
- Safe fields (run_id, timestamp, phase, event_type, scenario_id, seed) always preserved
- Internal fields (artifact_paths) redacted to basename only
- Sensitive fields (unknown context keys) hash-redacted with stable placeholders

#### 3. Replay Decoder
- `decode_jsonl_stream()` — JSONL text → typed `LogEventSchema` events with error capture
- `encode_jsonl_stream()` — typed events → JSONL text
- `verify_roundtrip()` — encode-decode-encode consistency verification
- `DecodedStream` with event/error counts, blank line tracking

### Test Evidence

**36 unit tests, all passing:**

```
cargo test -p fsqlite-harness --lib log_schema_validator
test result: ok. 36 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored
```

**Test breakdown by category:**

Field Classification (5 tests):
- field_classifications_cover_all_schema_fields
- required_fields_are_safe
- unknown_fields_default_sensitive
- known_safe_context_key_preserved
- artifact_paths_classified_internal

Redaction (7 tests):
- redaction_is_deterministic
- redaction_different_salt_different_output
- safe_fields_preserved_after_redaction
- invariant_ids_preserved_after_redaction
- artifact_paths_redacted_to_basename
- sensitive_context_keys_redacted
- path_redaction_handles_multiple_paths
- path_redaction_handles_no_directory

Replay Decoder (8 tests):
- decode_valid_jsonl
- decode_multiple_events
- decode_skips_blank_lines
- decode_captures_errors
- decode_mixed_valid_and_invalid
- roundtrip_canonical_events
- roundtrip_single_event
- roundtrip_empty_stream
- encode_produces_valid_jsonl

Batch Validation (11 tests):
- validate_canonical_examples_pass
- validate_empty_stream
- validate_detects_empty_run_id
- validate_detects_bad_timestamp
- validate_detects_missing_seed_for_failure
- validate_warns_on_missing_scenario_id
- validate_warns_artifact_generated_without_hash
- validate_warns_on_run_id_without_dashes
- validate_report_tracks_unique_run_ids
- validate_report_tracks_phases_and_types
- validate_report_render_summary
- validate_report_json_roundtrip

Integration Pipeline (2 tests):
- full_pipeline_decode_validate_redact (decode → validate → redact → re-validate → roundtrip)
- full_pipeline_determinism (two identical pipeline runs produce byte-identical output)

### Quality Gates
- `cargo fmt --check` — clean
- `cargo clippy -p fsqlite-harness --lib` — zero issues in log_schema_validator.rs (pre-existing issues in other modules)
- All 36 tests deterministic and reproducible

### Acceptance Criteria Coverage
1. Unit tests for schema validation and redaction determinism — YES (36 tests)
2. Round-trip decoder consistency checks — YES (3 roundtrip tests + pipeline integration)
3. Rich structured logs with correlation IDs and first-divergence markers — YES (extended validation checks)
4. CI failure diagnostics pinpointing schema violations — YES (ValidationReport with per-field diagnostics)
